source: CIS AMAZON ELASTIC KUBERNETES SERVICE (EKS) BENCHMARK V1.6.0
title: Ensure that all Namespaces have Network Policies defined

input_row.source: CIS AMAZON ELASTIC KUBERNETES SERVICE (EKS) BENCHMARK V1.6.0
input_row.title: Ensure that all Namespaces have Network Policies defined
input_row.description: Use network policies to isolate traffic in your cluster network.
input_row.rationale: Running different applications on the same Kubernetes cluster creates a risk of one compromised application attacking a neighboring application. Network segmentation is important to ensure that containers can communicate only with those they are supposed to. A network policy is a specification of how selections of pods are allowed to communicate with each other and other network endpoints. Once there is any Network Policy in a namespace selecting a particular pod, that pod will reject any connections that are not allowed by any Network Policy. Other pods in the namespace that are not selected by any Network Policy will continue to accept all traffic" Impact: Once there is any Network Policy in a namespace selecting a particular pod, that pod will reject any connections that are not allowed by any Network Policy. Other pods in the namespace that are not selected by any Network Policy will continue to accept all traffic"
input_row.audit: Run the below command and review the NetworkPolicy objects created in the cluster. kubectl get networkpolicy --all-namespaces Ensure that each namespace defined in the cluster has at least one Network Policy.
input_row.remediation: Follow the documentation and create NetworkPolicy objects as you need them. Default Value: By default, network policies are not created. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/services-networking/networkpolicies/ 2. https://octetz.com/posts/k8s-network-policy-apis 3. https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/configure-pod-container/declare-network-policy/
input_row.impact: Once there is any Network Policy in a namespace selecting a particular pod, that pod will reject any connections that are not allowed by any Network Policy. Other pods in the namespace that are not selected by any Network Policy will continue to accept all traffic"

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**APPROACH DECISION:**

The control "Ensure that all Namespaces have Network Policies defined" involves reviewing Kubernetes network policies within EKS clusters. The audit field specifies using the `kubectl` command to check for the presence of NetworkPolicy objects in each namespace. This verification relies on Kubernetes-specific configurations and is typically performed within the Kubernetes environment rather than directly through AWS APIs.

**DECISION CRITERIA:**

- This control requires the examination of Kubernetes-specific resources (NetworkPolicy objects) within namespaces.
- AWS APIs, boto3, or AWS CLI do not natively manage Kubernetes resources like NetworkPolicy objects.
- The task involves understanding the network policies defined within the Kubernetes context, which may require human interpretation and understanding of policy implications.
- AWS services focus on underlying infrastructure and permissions, not the application-layer constructs managed by Kubernetes specific tools like `kubectl`.

**MANUAL STEPS:**

1. **Access EKS Cluster:**
   - Use your configured IAM credentials with the necessary permissions to access the EKS cluster.
   - Ensure the `kubectl` tool is configured to interact with your EKS cluster.

2. **List Network Policies:**
   - Execute the command `kubectl get networkpolicy --all-namespaces`.
   - This lists all existing NetworkPolicy objects across all namespaces in the cluster.

3. **Verify Presence of Network Policies:**
   - Check that each namespace has at least one NetworkPolicy object defined.
   - Take note of any namespaces without defined NetworkPolicy objects, which represent a compliance issue.

4. **Expected Secure Configuration:**
   - Each namespace should have network policies that control traffic flow to and from pods in accordance with security requirements.

5. **Confirm Compliance:**
   - Document namespaces and respective Network Policies.
   - Report any namespaces lacking the expected network segmentation, indicating non-compliance with the control.
